Yes — a light jacket would be a good idea for Lisbon this Friday.

---
### 📅 **Friday, May 15**
    - 🌡️ **Temperature**: 13.0°C to 20.0°C
    - ☁️ **Conditions**: Sunny intervals
    - 💧 **Rain**: No rain expected (0.0%)
    - 💨 **Wind**: North, Moderate

💡 **Practical Tips**

- Mornings and evenings will feel cooler than midday, so a light layer is useful.
- With no rain expected, you likely won’t need waterproof gear.

📌 **Source:** [*IPMA*](https://www.ipma.pt/en/) | **Updated:** 09:22

benchmark_results.query: What are the best museums in Lisbon?
benchmark_results.response:
### 🔵 **Places and Attractions**
🧭 **Result window:** 1-5 of 5.

- **🏛️ Gulbenkian Museum**
    - 📝 **Description:** Closed for renovation until july'26 | Discover the collections, centre of modern art, bookshop, gardens and open-air auditorium where a very important part of Lisbon’s cultural life takes place.
    - 📂 **Category:** Museums
    - 📍 **Address:** [Av. Berna, 45-A, 1067-001, Lisboa](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Av.+Berna%2C+45-A%2C+1067-001%2C+Lisboa)
    - 💶 **Price:** 20% with Lisboa Card

    - ✉️ **Email:** [museu@gulbenkian.pt](mailto:museu@gulbenkian.pt)
    - 🌐 **Website:** [Official website](https://gulbenkian.pt/museu/en/)
    - 🔗 **More details:** [VisitLisboa](https://www.visitlisboa.com/en/places/gulbenkian-museum)

- **🏛️ CAM – Centro de Arte Moderna Gulbenkian**
    - 📝 **Description:** CAM presents an extensive renovation with new exhibition spaces, new galleries and a vast garden area, designed by Japanese architect Kengo Kuma, in collaboration with landscape architect Vladimir Djurovic.
    - 📂 **Category:** Museums & Monuments
    - 📍 **Address:** [Rua Marquês de Fronteira, 2, 1070-295, Lisboa](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Rua+Marqu%C3%AAs+de+Fronteira%2C+2%2C+1070-295%2C+Lisboa)
    - 💶 **Price:** 20% with Lisboa Card
    - ⭐ **Rating:** TripAdvisor 4/5 (248 reviews)
    - 📞 **Phone:** [+351 217 823 000](tel:+351 217 823 000)
    - ✉️ **Email:** [info@gulbenkian.pt](mailto:info@gulbenkian.pt)
    - 🌐 **Website:** [Official website](https://gulbenkian.pt/cam/en)
    - 🔗 **More details:** [VisitLisboa](https://www.visitlisboa.com/en/places/cam-centro-de-arte-moderna-gulbenkian)

- **🏛️ Maritime Museum**
    - 📝 **Description:** The Maritime Museum is a World of Discoveries. It reflects the importance of the experience and knowledge that the pioneering Portuguese navigators brought to the world.
    - 📂 **Category:** Museums
    - 📍 **Address:** [Praça do Império, 1400-206, Lisboa](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Pra%C3%A7a+do+Imp%C3%A9rio%2C+1400-206%2C+Lisboa)
    - 🕒 **Hours:** Today: 10:00 - 18:00
    - 💶 **Price:** 20% with Lisboa Card; Children free until age 3; Children (4-12): 4 €; Adult: 8 €; Family: 21 €; Senior: 4 €
    - 🎟️ **Tickets:** [Buy tickets](https://cultura.marinha.pt/pt/museu/planearvisita/bilhetes)
    - ⭐ **Rating:** TripAdvisor 4.3/5 (1367 reviews)
    - 📞 **Phone:** [+351 210 977 388](tel:+351210977388)
    - ✉️ **Email:** [geral.museu@marinha.pt](mailto:geral.museu@marinha.pt)
    - 🌐 **Website:** [Official website](https://cultura.marinha.pt/pt/museumarinha)
    - 🔗 **More details:** [VisitLisboa](https://www.visitlisboa.com/en/places/maritime-museum)

- **🏛️ São Vicente de Fora Museum**
    - 📝 **Description:** Come and discover this unique church and its history, which dates back to the reconquest of Lisbon and the centuries that followed right up to today.
    - 📂 **Category:** Museums
    - 📍 **Address:** [Largo de São Vicente, 1100, Lisboa](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Largo+de+S%C3%A3o+Vicente%2C+1100%2C+Lisboa)

    - 📞 **Phone:** [+351 218 810 559](tel:+351218810559)
    - ✉️ **Email:** [museu@patriarcado-lisboa.pt](mailto:museu@patriarcado-lisboa.pt)
    - 🌐 **Website:** [Official website](http://www.patriarcado-lisboa.pt/)
    - 🔗 **More details:** [VisitLisboa](https://www.visitlisboa.com/en/places/sao-vicente-de-fora-museum)

- **🏛️ National Tile Museum**
    - 📝 **Description:** The museum is set in Madre de Deus Convent, founded in 1509 and its collections allow a journey through the history of tile, from 15th century till present days.

    - 🔗 **More details:** [VisitLisboa](https://www.visitlisboa.com/en/places/national-tile-museum)

- **🎵 James Taylor Quartet**

benchmark_results.query: Tell me about the history of Castelo de São Jorge
benchmark_results.response:
- 📚 **Historical context: Tell me about the history of Castelo de São Jorge**

- The castle hill has been occupied since ancient periods and became strategically important during Islamic rule in Lisbon.
- The medieval fortification developed as an alcáçova and citadel in the Islamic period, with important references from around the mid-11th century.
- After the conquest of Lisbon in 1147, it became a symbolic seat of royal power and part of the palatial area used by the Portuguese court.
- As court life later shifted toward the riverfront, the castle lost palatial centrality, but its walls, views, and archaeological remains still reveal several layers of Lisbon's urban history.

📌 **Source:** [*Wikipedia/Web*](https://www.wikipedia.org/) | **Updated:** 09:42

🌐 **Resultados Tavily Search para 'Castelo de São Jorge Lisboa Portugal':**

### ✅ authoritative [en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Jorge_Castle)
São Jorge Castle (Portuguese: Castelo de São Jorge; Portuguese pronunciation: (/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese")), sometimes known in English as Saint George's Castle, is a historic castle in the Portuguese capital of Lisbon, located in the freguesia of Santa Maria Maior "Santa Maria Maior (Lisbon)"). Human occupation of the castle hill dates to at least the 8th century BC while the oldest fortifications on the site date from the 2nd century BC. The hill on which Saint George's Castle stands has played an important part in the history of Lisbon, having served as the location of fortifications occupied successively by Phoenicians, Carthaginians, Romans, and Moors, before its conquest by the Portuguese in the 1147 Siege of Lisbon. Since the 12th century, the castle has [...] | Castelo de São Jorge |
| Lisbon in Portugal |
| The visible profile of Saint George's Castle overlooking the historical center of Lisbon. |
| Site information |
| Type | Castle |
| Owner | Government of Portugal |
| Operator | Cmara Municipal de Lisboa |
| Open to the public | Public |
| Website | castelodesaojorge.pt |
| Location |
| São Jorge Castle Saint George's Castle is located in Lisbon São Jorge Castle Saint George's Castle  São Jorge Castle   Saint George's Castle  Location of the castle within the municipality of Lisbon |
| Coordinates | 38°42′50″N 9°08′01″W﻿ / ﻿38.71389°N 9.13361°W﻿ / 38.71389; -9.13361 |
| Site history |
| Built by | Kings of Portugal, most notably Ferdinand I, Denis and Sebastian João Fernandes and Vasco Brás | [...] The castle's plan is roughly square, and it was originally encircled by a wall, to form a citadel. The castle complex consists of the castle itself (the castelejo), some ancillary buildings (including the ruins of the royal palace), gardens, and a large terraced square from which an impressive panorama of Lisbon is visible. The main entrance to the citadel is a 19th-century gate surmounted by the coat-of-arms of Portugal, the name of Queen Maria II, and the date 1846. This gate permits access to the main square (Praça d'Armas), which is decorated with old cannons and a bronze statue of Afonso Henriques, the Portuguese monarch who took the castle from the Moors. This statue is a copy of the 19th-century original, by the romantic sculptor António Soares dos Reis, which is located near

### 🌍 web [castelodesaojorge.pt](https://castelodesaojorge.pt/en/castle/national-monument/the-castelo-de-sao-jorge/)
During the Islamic occupation, the city was defended by lines of walls which were positioned on the south side of the hill towards the river. The layout of these walls coincided roughly with the walls of the Roman city of Olisipo (Lisbon), Within these walls was the medina or city and a line of walls encircling the hilltop where the citadel (kasbah) was located, with its the great residential centre of religious, political, military administrative and social power.kasbah) was located, with its the great residential centre of religious, political, military administrative and social power.

It was here that the medieval castle we know today was erected in the period following the reconquest of Lisbon. [...] Where the western wall and the southern wall meet, there is a section of wall that goes down the slope, at the top of which stands the Torre de São Lourenço (St. Lawrence’s Tower), associated with a city gate that has now disappeared.

This section of wall, as well as the one that delimits the northern part of the Praça Nova (New Square), are part of later modifications (dating from 1373) instigated by King Ferdinand for the defence of the city. The objective of this largescale project was to provide the city of Lisbon (which had expanded to the west and north) with a new and wider ring of walls to protect it. This fence, in memory of the monarch who built it, is now known as the Fernandine Wall (Cerca Fernandina).

## Gateways [...] We do not know, however, what the fortification and citadel would have looked like during the period of Islamic rule or at the time of the first Christian kings.

The appearance of the castle as it looks today (bearing characteristics attributable to “the gothic castle”) is the result of changes occurring during the reign of King Afonso III (1248-1279) and/or King Denis (1279-1325): a walled quadrangular perimeter, fortified by 11 towers and divided into two places-of-arms; wide wall-walks; the keep (which has not survived, but which was probably over the gateway and attached to the wall); the barbican (a lower advanced wall built thicker at its base) in the eastern and southern areas, which was intended to defend the fortress’s most vulnerable points; and a moat and Albarrana towers.

### 🌍 web [castelodesaojorge.pt](https://castelodesaojorge.pt/en/castle/national-monument/history/origins-and-development/)
Although it is widely accepted that there was a fortified city with a kasbah, there remain many doubts about the existence of a castle and the records left by twelfth-century crusaders and Muslim geographers are silent on this matter.

The oldest references to a castle within the walled perimeter of the kasbah date back to the time of D. Afonso III, the monarch who introduced the principles of active defence in Portugal, which are present in this fortress, and of his son, D. Dinis. [...] It is possible that the Islamic settlement on the hill dates back to the 8th century AD and that the existing city was fortified soon after. Largescale construction work took place in Lisbon during the Umayyad period, more specifically in the time of Hisam al-Mu’ayyad bi-lllãh. According to a commemorative inscription dated to 985, these were undertaken by Almanzor. This inscription uses the term madina when referring to Lisbon, which means walled city. Thus, it can be deduced that, at least from the 10th century, the city of Lisbon was protected by a large wall that had been severely damaged and was in urgent need of repair. [...] The castle we see today is, without doubt, a gothic castle; it features a quadrangular layout, an elbow shaped gateway, 10 quadrangular turrets attached to the castle wall and a barbican in the more vulnerable south-eastern area, with a thickened base and moat, featuring medieval drawings and inscriptions, and the existence of Albarrana towers.

The fortress underwent extensive modifications over time; transformative buildings work took place during the reigns of King Afonso Henriques (following the conquest of the city), King Afonso III, King Denis (a well-documented period), King Ferdinand, and even during the reigns of King John I and Afonso V. King Manuel and King Sebastian also oversaw extensive modifications to the fortification.

### 🌍 web [lisbon.net](https://www.lisbon.net/sao-jorge-castle)
Civitatis   Lisbon

São Jorge Castle

1. Things to Do
2. Landmarks and Tourist Attractions

# São Jorge Castle

Located on the peak of the São Jorge hill, Lisbon's highest, stands the magnificent São Jorge Castle, one of the most historic symbols of the city.

São Jorge Castle (Castelo de São Jorge) is one of Lisbon’s most iconic landmarks. The silhouette of the Castle stands out on the summit of São Jorge hill, the highest in Lisbon, both by day and when illuminated at night. The hillside also houses two of the city’s neighbourhoods called Alfama and Castelo. [...] ## Visiting São Jorge Castle

The visit to São Jorge Castle will take approximately the whole morning since the site is extensive. Besides the Castle with its eleven towers, you’ll also be able to visit a small museum, a bar, and a restaurant if you want to stop for lunch before you continue exploring Lisbon. After your visit, we recommend you take a walk along some of the surrounding streets.

Make the most of your visit to São Jorge Castle by visiting the Lisbon Cathedral, which is just a few minutes away by foot.

The best views of the east of Lisbon await you at the top of the Fortress.

São Jorge Castle

Entrance to São Jorge Castle

Views from São Jorge Castle

Panoramic views from São Jorge Castle

2See photo gallery

## Location

At the top of São Jorge Hill.

## Schedule [...] This small fortress was built by the Visigoths during the fifth century. It was enlarged by the Moors in the ninth century and modified during the reign of Alfonso I of Portugal (1109 – 1185). In later years, it was transformed into a Royal Palace. Finally, it was completely restored in 1938.

The Castle reached its maximum splendor during the thirteenth century and lasted until the sixteenth century when it was occupied by both the Kings of Portugal and the Bishop.

Only a few years ago, Phoenician, Greek, and Carthaginian ruins were found in the area, and are currently being uncovered.

## Visiting São Jorge Castle

### 🌍 web [lisbonheritagehotels.com](https://lisbonheritagehotels.com/sao-jorge-castle/)
### São Jorge Castle: the history

This is one of the most famous castles in Portugal. It stands on the highest of the Seven Hills of Lisbon and was part of the noble area of the medieval citadel, which, besides the castle, included the royal palace (Paço Real) and residential quarters of the elite, whose houses are still visible on the Archaeological Site.

Dating back to the mid-11th century, it was built by the Muslims and later taken by D. Afonso Henriques, in the conquest of Lisbon, on 25 October 1147. Until the early 16th century, this castle was the most important venue for the Portuguese court and where several kings were acclaimed between the 14th and 16th centuries.Find out more about D. Afonso Henriques. [...] Archaeological research in the surrounding areas continued throughout the following decades, gradually revealing the priceless historical value that justified the classification of São Jorge Castle as a National Monument, through the Royal Charter of 1910.

Check out the archaeological objects on display at Solar do Castelo. Some of the most important findings can be seen at the Permanent Exhibition on the Archaeological Site. Find out more about São Jorge Castle here.

## Don't Miss Out On:

Courtyard of Solar do Castelo with tables and chairs

#### Heritage Solar do Castelo – The experience of living in a Castle [...] Besides being the residence of kings, the court and the bishop, the castle was also used to install the royal archives, to welcome distinguished national and international figures, both as a royal palace, but also as a military stronghold between 1580 and the early 20th century.

In 1755, after the Lisbon earthquake, the venue underwent several reconstructions that ended up concealing part of the Castle’s past and left it only with the military function, occupied with barracks.

In the 1930s, there was the greatest investment ever made in this National Monument, which underwent renovations that made it possible to rediscover the castle, the historical remains and ancient buildings. The castle lost its military function and was finally returned to the citizens.

### 🔵 **Places and Attractions**
🧭 **Result window:** 1-5 of 5.

- **🏛️ Gulbenkian Museum**
    - 📝 **Description:** Closed for renovation until july'26 | Discover the collections, centre of modern art, bookshop, gardens and open-air auditorium where a very important part of Lisbon’s cultural life takes place.
    - 📂 **Category:** Museums
    - 📍 **Address:** [Av. Berna, 45-A, 1067-001, Lisboa](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Av.+Berna%2C+45-A%2C+1067-001%2C+Lisboa)

benchmark_results.query: Tell me about the history of Castelo de São Jorge
benchmark_results.response:
- 📚 **Historical context: Tell me about the history of Castelo de São Jorge**

- The castle hill has been occupied since ancient periods and became strategically important during Islamic rule in Lisbon.
- The medieval fortification developed as an alcáçova and citadel in the Islamic period, with important references from around the mid-11th century.
- After the conquest of Lisbon in 1147, it became a symbolic seat of royal power and part of the palatial area used by the Portuguese court.
- As court life later shifted toward the riverfront, the castle lost palatial centrality, but its walls, views, and archaeological remains still reveal several layers of Lisbon's urban history.

📌 **Source:** [*Wikipedia/Web*](https://www.wikipedia.org/) | **Updated:** 10:08

[search_history_culture] returned:
📚 **Wikipédia: São Jorge Castle**
🔗 URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Jorge_Castle

São Jorge Castle, sometimes known in English as Saint George's Castle, is a historic castle in the Portuguese capital of Lisbon, located in the freguesia of Santa Maria Maior. Human occupation of the castle hill dates to at least the 8th century BC while the oldest fortifications on the site date from the 2nd century BC. The hill on which Saint George's Castle stands has played an important part in the history of Lisbon, having served as the location of fortifications occupied successively by Phoenicians, Carthaginians, Romans, and Moors, before its conquest by the Portuguese in the 1147 Siege of Lisbon. Since the 12th century, the castle has variously served as a royal palace, a military barracks, home of the Torre do Tombo National Archive, and now as a national monument and museum.

✅ **Yes, but as a discount:** Oceanário de Lisboa is listed with **15% with Lisboa Card**, not free entry.

- 📍 **Address:** [Esplanada D. Carlos I, 1990-005, Lisboa](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Esplanada+D.+Carlos+I%2C+1990-005%2C+Lisboa)
- 🌐 **More details:** [VisitLisboa](https://www.visitlisboa.com/en/places/oceanario-de-lisboa)

benchmark_results.query: I'd like a few quiet lesser-known places in Lisbon.
benchmark_results.expected_behavior: Recommend real, verified places only and explicitly avoid guessing when data is limited.
benchmark_results.response:
Here are some quiet, lesser-known spots in Lisbon that offer a peaceful escape from the crowds:

- **🌿 Miradouro do Torel**
    - 📝 **Description:** One of Lisbon's best-kept secrets, this viewpoint offers unique beauty and a tranquil atmosphere away from the tourist-heavy miradouros.
    - 📂 **Category:** View Points
    - 📍 **Address:** [Rua Júlio de Andrade, Lisboa](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Rua+J%C3%BAlio+de+Andrade%2C+Lisboa)
    - ⭐ **Rating:** TripAdvisor 4.1/5 (141 reviews)

- **🏛️ Galeria Subterrânea do Loreto**
    - 📝 **Description:** Discover the underground side of Lisbon in these fascinating galleries beneath the streets. A hidden world that most visitors never see.
    - 📂 **Category:** Monuments
    - 📍 **Address:** [Praça do Príncipe Real, 1250-096, Lisboa](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Pra%C3%A7a+do+Pr%C3%ADncipe+Real%2C+1250-096%2C+Lisboa)
    - ⭐ **Rating:** TripAdvisor 4.2/5 (201 reviews)
    - 📞 **Phone:** [+351 218 100 215](tel:+351218100215)
    - 🌐 **Website:** [Official website](http://www.museudaagua.blogspot.pt)

- **🏛️ Aqueduto das Águas Livres**
    - 📝 **Description:** This giant stone aqueduct crosses Lisbon and offers a fascinating glimpse into the city's engineering history. Far less visited than major monuments, it withstood the 1755 earthquake.
    - 📂 **Category:** Monuments
    - 📍 **Address:** [Travessia do Vale de Alcântara - Calçada da Quintinha, nº 6 (Campolide), Lisboa](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Travessia+do+Vale+de+Alc%C3%A2ntara+-+Cal%C3%A7ada+da+Quintinha%2C+n%C2%BA+6+%28Campolide%29%2C+Lisboa)
    - 💶 **Price:** 50% with Lisboa Card
    - ⭐ **Rating:** TripAdvisor 4.3/5 (299 reviews)
    - 📞 **Phone:** [+351 218 100 215](tel:+351218100215)
    - 🌐 **Website:** [Official website](http://museudaagua.blogspot.pt)

- **🏛️ Water Museum - Barbadinhos Steam Pumping Station**
    - 📝 **Description:** A collection of 18th and 19th-century monuments representing an important chapter in Lisbon's water supply history. Quiet and educational.
    - 📂 **Category:** Monuments
    - 📍 **Address:** [Rua do Alviela, 12, 1170-012, Lisboa](https://www.google.com/maps/search/?api=1&query=Rua+do+Alviela%2C+12%2C+1170-012%2C+Lisboa)
    - 🕒 **Hours:** Today: Closed
    - 💶 **Price:** 50% with Lisboa Card
    - ⭐ **Rating:** TripAdvisor 3.8/5 (62 reviews)
    - 📞 **Phone:** [+351 218 100 215](tel:+351218100215)
    - 🌐 **Website:** [Official website](http://www.epal.pt)

**🌳 Monsanto Cycle Path**
- 📝 **Description:** Discover a side of Lisbon that few know: the natural side. This leafy 900-hectare park is Lisbon's lung, offering green tranquility away from the city bustle.
- 📂 **Category:** Running/Nature

📌 **Source:** [*VisitLisboa Places*](https://www.visitlisboa.com/en/places) | **Updated:** 10:10

benchmark_results.query: Quais são as melhores praias perto de Lisboa?
benchmark_results.response:
Aqui estão as melhores praias perto de Lisboa, organizadas por zona:

---

### 🏖️ **Costa da Caparica** (Almada)

A cerca de 20 minutos de carro do centro de Lisboa (atravessando a Ponte 25 de Abril). São 15 quilómetros de costa com areais para todos os gostos.

- **Praia da Rainha** – Praia familiar, frequentada por colónias de férias. Conhecida pela pesca xávega tradicional.
- **Praia do Castelo** – Mais tranquila, ideal para quem procura sossego.
- **Praia da Fonte da Telha** – No extremo sul, mais selvagem e menos concorrida.

*Como chegar:* Carro (recomendado), autocarro desde Lisboa, ou barco+autocarro. Cerca de 1 hora em transportes públicos.

---

### 🌊 **Cascais** (Linha de Cascais)

A cerca de 30 minutos de comboio desde o Cais do Sodré. A linha férrea passa junto à costa, com paragens perto das praias.

- **Praia da Conceição** – A mais próxima da estação de comboios de Cascais. Águas calmas, ideal para famílias. Liga-se à Praia da Duquesa pelo paredão.
- **Praia da Poça** – Enseada pitoresca na Riviera Portuguesa, mar tranquilo.
- **Praia do Guincho** – Para surf, kitesurf e windsurf. Localizada no Parque Natural de Sintra-Cascais, com paisagem mais selvagem e vento forte.

---

### 🌴 **Estoril** (Linha de Cascais)

- **Praia do Tamariz** – Uma das mais procuradas, situada em frente ao famoso Casino do Estoril. Tem piscina oceânica gratuita e excelentes acessos (estação de comboios e autocarros mesmo ao lado).
- **Praia do Monte Estoril** – Continuação da Praia do Tamariz, mais tranquila.

---

### 🏄 **Carcavelos** (Oeiras)

Entre Lisboa e Cascais, acessível também pela linha de comboio.
- Famosa pelo surf e bodyboard. muito popular entre jovens e desportistas.

- **De comboio:** A linha Lisboa–Cascais (partida do Cais do Sodré) é a melhor opção para as praias de Cascais e Estoril, com paragens junto à costa.
- **De carro:** Recomendado para a Costa da Caparica e para praias mais remotas como o Guincho. Em Cascais, o estacionamento é limitado e o trânsito pode ser complicado nos dias de verão.
- **Para surf:** Guincho, Carcavelos e Costa da Caparica são as melhores opções.
- **Para famílias:** Praia da Conceição (Cascais) e Praia do Tamariz (Estoril) têm águas mais calmas e bons acessos.

# Melhores praias de Cascais, Carcavelos e Estoril para 2026

Praia da Poça | Melhores praias de Cascais e do Estoril  

Praia da Poça | Melhores praias de Cascais e do Estoril

 ByPedro Domingues
 Janeiro 12, 2025

Share this content!

2 shares

 Facebook
 Twitter
 Email

Cascais é um destino de praia a poucos quilómetros de Lisboa, com um charme tão singular que atrai ano após ano visitantes de todo o mundo. Afinal, falamos do coração da Riviera Portuguesa, também conhecida como Costa do Sol, repleta de enseadas pitorescas com praias de mar tranquilo, todas elas de muito fácil acesso e rodeadas de imensa infraestrutura de apoio. [...] ## Como chegar a Cascais?

Para facilitar toda a tua viagem, não necessitarás de carro para a tua estadia em Cascais, a não ser que pretendas mesmo ir visitar o Parque Natural de Sintra-Cascais e toda a região do Guincho. Se assim for, e caso não tenhas viatura própria, utiliza a Discover Cars para as tuas pesquisas e reservas de automóvel.

É muito fácil visitar as melhores praias de Cascais, Carcavelos e Estoril de comboio, a partir de Lisboa. Como poderás ver na imagem em baixo, existe uma linha de comboio que faz ligação direta com o centro de Lisboa (Cais do Sodré), pelo que a poderás utilizar para visitar as praias da região, já que o trajeto é bem junto da costa. Sabe mais sobre horários e preços no site da CP – Comboios de Portugal.

Linha de Comboios Lisboa – Cascais

### 🌍 web [lisbonbeachesguide.com](https://www.lisbonbeachesguide.com/praias/top-10-melhores-praias-de-lisboa.html)
De carro: O carro é a melhor opção para chegar às praias mais remotas da região, como as de Sintra ou da Arrábida. Para as praias da Costa da Caparica é também o mais recomendado, uma vez que para chegar às melhores (para sul) através de transportes públicos, envolve dois meios de transporte ou uma grande caminhada. Para Cascais poderá não ser a melhor opção, uma vez que o estacionamento aqui é bastante limitado e o trnsito pode ser caótico. Além disso, o comboio desta linha tem paragens próximas das praias principais. [...] Perfeita para: Surf, Kitesurf, Animação Noturna, Naturismo   
 Vigiada: Sim   
 WC: Sim   
 Duches: Sim

 Bar ou Restaurante: Sim   
 Acessível: Sim   
 Estacionamento: Sim   
 Como chegar a partir de Lisboa: Carro, Autocarro ou Barco+Autocarro   
 Tempo de viagem do centro de Lisboa: 20 minutos de carro e cerca de 1 hora de transportes públicos   
 Onde Ficar: Tryp Lisboa Caparica Mar ou Aroeira Lisbon Hotel

Para mais informações, consulte o guia da Costa da Caparica.

  
 4. Praia do Guincho
Praia do Guincho, Cascais [...] 1. Praia da Conceição
Praia da Conceição, Cascais

É a praia mais próxima da estação de comboios de Cascais, mas são as suas águas calmas e a bela paisagem que atraem os turistas e os jovens que a frequentam. Com uma localização perfeita, mesmo ao lado do centro histórico de Cascais, a Praia da Conceição é onde começa o paredão de Cascais, um percurso de quase três quilómetros que termina na Praia da Azarujinha, passando por várias outras praias. Se procura momentos de tranquilidade à beira-mar e muito espaço para estender a toalha, esta não será a praia ideal para si, pois normalmente encontra-se lotada. No entanto, merece sempre uma visita e é um excelente ponto de partida para explorar as outras praias da costa mais próxima de Lisboa.

### 🌍 web [timeout.pt](https://www.timeout.pt/lisboa/pt/coisas-para-fazer/praias-da-costa-da-caparica)
Da Praia de São João à Fonte da Telha, a Costa da Caparica oferece areais para todos os gostos – dos mais concorridos aos recantos menos óbvios. Ao longo de 15 quilómetros, há muito por onde escolher, mas quando o calor aperta e os lisboetas atravessam a ponte, os melhores espaços para estender a toalha tornam-se bastante disputados. Isto pode desmotivar (temos sugestões para quem prefere uma alternativa à praia – sim, estamos a olhar para si que prefere nadar nas piscinas)–, ainda assim, e mesmo com todo o trnsito, vale a pena rumar à outra margem: além de praias com bandeira azul, não faltam bons restaurantes e esplanadas onde parar para uma refeição ou simplesmente apreciar o pôr-do-sol. Pegue no saco – não se esqueça do seu livro favorito – escolha o areal certo, coloque o protector [...] livro favorito – escolha o areal certo, coloque o protector solar e desfrute do dia. [...] ### Praia da Rainha

Construções de luxo, palácios e grandes hotéis guardam esta praia, que é a continuação da praia do Monte Estoril.  
  
Um sol brilhante quase todo o ano é uma tentação também para jogar golfe, tal como percorrer as atracções naturais dos arredores, participar de competições de kitesurf ou windsurf, talvez presenciar alguma das partidas do Estoril Open, prova oficial da ATP, viver as suas noites intensas, presenciar o grande prémio de motociclismo ou automobilismo ou apenas simplesmente saborear os peixes e mariscos. Estoril é uma importante zona costeira de Portugal perto de Lisboa.  
  
A costa de Estoril tem bom clima durante quase todo o ano, sem condições meteorológicas extremas. Graças às proximidades com a Serra de Sintra a paisagem é colorida e rica em fauna e flora.  
  
A estrada marginal serpenteia pela costa, numa bela viagem até ao Estoril. Também pode escolher viajar de comboio, que parte do Cais do Sodré em Lisboa, numa linha construída em 1889 e que era usada pelos aristocratas de Portugal nas suas visitas terapêuticas às praias da zona.

# Cervejaria Ramiro Lisbon Review: Menu, Pricing, And Info

Posted on  by Amber Hoffman

It’s rare for a restaurant that tops a must-visit list to live up to the hype. But every time we visit Lisbon, Portugal, we visit Cervejaria Ramiro Lisboa. Sometimes twice. Not only does Cervejaria Ramiro Lisbon offer some of the best seafood in Lisbon, but it is a Lisbon institution.

In this post, we share the must-eat dishes from the Cervejaria Ramiro menu and what to expect from dining at O Ramiro.

### Cervejaria Ramiro Restaurant – Lisbon, Portugal

Address: Av. Almirante Reis nº1 – H, 1150-007 Lisboa, Portugal  
 Open hours: 12 pm – 12:30 am on Tuesday – Sunday (closed on Mondays)  
 Website:   
 Prices: Our total bill came to 108.42 Euros for everything  
 How to get there: Cervejaria Ramiro Restaurant is located along Av. Almirante Reis in Lisbon, Portugal. You can get there from either Intendente or Martim Moniz Metro stations. My wife and I walked from the central Rossio square area, and it only took about 15 minutes.

## Cervejaria Ramiro Restaurant

I was looking forward to eating at a lot of restaurants during my trip to Lisbon, Portugal.

But nothing got me more excited than thinking about Cervejaria Ramiro Restaurant, known to serve some of Lisbon’s best seafood. [...] ## Conclusion

Some say the seas off of Portugal are home to the best seafood in the world. So it goes without saying that you’ll eat a lot of amazing seafood when you’re in Lisbon, and there are plenty of good restaurants to choose from.

Cervejaria Ramiro is famous for both locals and tourists because they serve honest high quality, fresh, and extraordinary seafood at fair and reasonable prices, with friendly service. That’s the formula that works, and which has made them one of the best seafood restaurants in Lisbon.

The seafood was extraordinary, and the entire meal experience, made it one of the best seafood feasts I’ve ever had!
